data.title: Lithium Miners News For The Month Of January 2026
data.description:
Welcome to the January 2026 edition of the lithium miner news.

The past month saw lithium prices surge significantly higher, boosted by very strong battery energy stationary storage ("BESS") demand and supply concerns.

Lithium price news

Metal.com reported a lithium spodumene concentrate index (Li2O 5.5%-6.2%, excluding tax/insurance/freight) spot price of USD 2,035 as of January 21, 2026, surging higher the past month.

The China lithium carbonate spot price surged ~60% higher last month, now at CNY 158,500. The CIF Asia lithium carbonate price is ~US$11.38/kg.

China lithium carbonate spot price 5-year chart - CNY 158,500 (~USD 22,760) (source)

Lithium demand versus supply outlook

Katusa Research is forecasting a small lithium deficit to begin in 2026 (as of January 2026) (source)

RMIS forecasts lithium deficits to start after 2028 and may grow very large without major new investments (as of May 2025) (source)

Albemarle is forecasting lithium deficits more likely from ~2027/28 (as of mid-2025) (source)

GGII, a Shenzhen-based consultancy, forecasts a balanced lithium market this year, a 10,000t LCE deficit in 2026, and a 60,000t deficit in 2027 (as of mid-2025) (source)

Trend Investing vs. IEA Demand Forecast for EV Metals (IEA)

Lithium market and battery news

On December 24, CNEVPOST reported:

CATL's key lithium mine expected to resume production around Chinese New Year. CATL suspended operations at the Jianxiawo lithium mine on August 10 due to the expiration of a key mining permit.

On December 25, CNEVPOST reported:

CATL signs 50-GWh energy storage deal with Chinese electrical equipment maker Sieyuan...In the first half of 2025, CATL's energy storage battery systems contributed RMB 28.4 billion in revenue, accounting for 15.87 percent of its total revenue of RMB 178.9 billion, according to its interim report.

On December 28, CarNewsChina reported:

CATL confirms 2026 large-scale sodium-ion battery deployment in multiple sectors...The company described expanded applications in battery swap systems, passenger vehicles, commercial vehicles, and energy storage, indicating a significant commercial deployment phase for sodium-ion technology next year.

On January 2, The West Australian reported:

Chariot MOU eyes 200ktpa Nigeria lithium offtake to China. Chariot Resources Limited has executed a non-binding memorandum of understanding (MOU) with Shanghai GreatPower Nickel & Cobalt Materials Co. Ltd., a prominent China-based battery materials producer, to explore collaboration across its prospective Nigerian lithium portfolio. The MOU outlines potential offtake for up to 200,000 tonnes per annum of spodumene concentrate - the principal ore of lithium - from one of Chariot's four Nigerian projects and included options for development financing, credit lines, and offtake prepayments to accelerate exploration and production.

On January 7, CNEVPOST reported, "Global EV battery market share in Jan-Nov 2025: CATL 38.2%, BYD 16.7%."

On January 7, Fastmarkets reported:

Lithium: Prices surge amid supply squeeze and speculative frenzy. This explosive surge is not just a story of numbers but of mounting supply-side tension: persistent delays to the reopening of CATL's Jianxiawo lepidolite mine, facility maintenance at other producers, and a scramble to secure inventories for long-term contracts have all combined to squeeze availability. The feverish price action has been further stoked by speculative trading, as bullish sentiment sweeps through the market... Spodumene prices surge, sparking potential Australian mine restarts...Lithium prices appear to have moved ahead of the fundamentals, propelled by speculative buying, bullish sentiment and a backdrop of heightened geopolitical risk. Yet we may also be finally witnessing demand catch up with the supply surge of recent years. The key takeaway is to brace for more volatility - this is a market where a single headline, project delay or policy shift can rewrite the outlook overnight.

On January 12, Seeking Alpha reported:

Lithium prices surge as China set to revoke battery export tax rebates... China's finance ministry said Friday that VAT export rebates for battery products would be cut to 6% from 9% starting in April and will be rolled back entirely from January 1, 2027.

On January 14, Trading Economics posted:

Lithium carbonate futures surged past CNY 150,000 per tonne in January, gaining nearly 30% since the start of the year to a two-year high, as signs of strong demand for power storage coincided with the outlook of capped supply. Chinese authorities lowered export rebates for battery producers from April, driving manufacturers to front-run lithium orders. In the meantime, ambitious bets in power and datacenter infrastructure by the Chinese government was combined with the announcement of higher power storage spending, supporting the outlook for lithium and other battery metals. This was combined with Beijing stating it would double EV charging capacity to 180 gigawatts by 2027, supporting lithium-rich energy storage systems. Meanwhile, authorities stated 27 mining permits were canceled in the lithium hub of Jiangxi, aligned with the earlier suspension of activity in CATL's Jianxiawo lithium mine amid Beijing's anti-involution campaign.

On January 14, CNEVPOST reported:

CATL signs $17.2 billion lithium order as world's largest EV battery maker secures key material supply. Ronbay will supply CATL with 3.05 million tons of LFP cathode material from the first quarter of 2026 through 2031.

Note: Ronbay is Ningbo Ronbay New Energy Technology [SHA:688005].

On January 15, Seeking Alpha reported: "Trump holds off on critical minerals tariffs..."

On January 15, Direct Lithium Extraction reported:

Argentina sets out 10-year plan for lithium growth...Officials say the objective is to raise output steadily over the next decade, with government projections pointing to a possible doubling of national production capacity by the early 2030s if priority projects proceed as planned.

On January 16 Miningdotcom reported:

Lithium swings from glut to scarcity on Asia demand, Traxys says... "We see a very healthy Asian demand and an under-supplied market at this point in time," said Martim Facada, managing director for lithium trading at Luxembourg-based Traxys. "We think the market has legs to keep going up."

Battery Energy Stationary Storage ("BESS") is a new, very strong driver of lithium demand (source)

On January 16, CNEVPOST reported:

China EV battery installations rise 40.4% to 769.7 GWh in 2025. LFP battery installations reached 625.3 GWh in 2025, accounting for 81.2% of total installations and growing 52.9% year-on-year. Power battery exports reached 189.7 GWh in 2025, accounting for 62.2% of total exports and marking a 41.9% year-on-year increase...China's combined production of power and energy storage batteries in December reached 201.7 GWh, up 62.1% year-on-year and up 14.4% month-on-month.

On January 16, Reuters reported:

US lawmakers introduce bill to create $2.5 billion critical-minerals stockpile...a move aimed at stabilizing market prices and encouraging domestic mining and refining. The bill marks Washington's latest attempt to offset what policymakers view as Chinese manipulation of prices for lithium, nickel, rare earths and other critical minerals - vital for items from electric vehicles to high-tech weaponry - that has stymied American mining companies for years.

Lithium miner news

Albemarle (ALB)

No significant news for the month; however, they reported that Q4 2025 earnings results are due for release on February 11.

Sociedad Quimica y Minera S.A. (SQM), Covalent Lithium (SQM/WES JV) - Wesfarmers [ASX:WES] (OTCPK:WFAFY)

The Mt Holland Lithium Mine and Kwinana Refinery are a 50/50 JV ("Covalent Lithium") between Wesfarmers and SQM, located in Western Australia.

On December 27, SQM announced: "The completion of the association agreement with Codelco."

On January 14, SQM announced:

Issue and sale of us$600 million subordinated capital notes...The notes will have an initial annual interest rate of 5.625%, and are subject to reset on April 22, 2031 (the "First Reset Date"), and on every fifth anniversary thereafter, with interest rate step-ups on the fifth and twentieth anniversaries of the First Reset Date...SQM intends to allocate the net proceeds from the offering of the notes to refinance its outstanding indebtedness and for general corporate purposes, including funding its capital expenditures program.

Rio Tinto [ASX:RIO] [LN:RIO] (RIO) - Acquired Arcadium Lithium in early 2025

On January 21, Rio Tinto announced:

Fourth quarter 2025 production results. Standout production results with +8% CuEq production growth in 2025...At Simandou, we celebrated the major milestone of first shipment from the port; a testament to our ability to deliver major growth projects..."In lithium, we achieved production growth from our operations and in-flight projects as planned in 2025, as we build out our high-quality portfolio with discipline.

Rio Tinto's 2025 lithium production table shows 57kt LCE (includes the contribution from Arcadium) (source: Fourth Quarter Operations Review 2025)

Note: 2026 lithium production guidance was given as 61-64kt LCE.

Note: Rio Tinto posted a response to the news released by Glencore of talks of a Rio Tinto merger with Glencore, etc. There was also news of a possible collaboration between Rio and BHP to mine up to 200 million tons of iron ore.

Upcoming catalysts include:

  • 2026 - Plan to expand and ramp up the Fenix Mine by 10,000tpa and start and ramp the Sal de Vida Project to 15,000tpa LCE.
  • 2028 - Plans to start ramping up Rincon to 60,000tpa LCE and start the Nemaska Lithium Project, then ramp to 28,000tpa LCE.

Note: You can view a nice summary of all Rio's lithium projects and timelines here. Their end-2028 target is 225,000tpa LCE.

Ganfeng Lithium Group [SHE:002460] [HK:1772] (GNENY)

On January 21, Market Screener reported:

JPMorgan Chase & Co's long position in H-shares of Ganfeng Lithium increases to 6.53% on Jan 15 from 5.81% - HKEX.

(Chengdu) Tianqi Lithium Industries Inc. [SHE:002466], Tianqi Lithium Energy Australia (TLEA) is a JV with Tianqi Lithium (51%) and IGO Limited (49%). TLEA owns the Kwinana lithium hydroxide facility in WA

On January 9, Market Screener announced: "WestStar Industrial says SIMPEC awarded contract by Tianqi Lithium Kwinana."

Chengxin Lithium Group Co., Ltd. [SHE:002240] - Also owns Sichuan Zhiyuan Lithium Industry Co., Ltd. and is a supplier to BYD Co.

On January 13, Market Screener announced: "Chengxin Lithium sees 2025 net loss at 600-850 million yuan."

Pilbara Minerals [ASX:PLS] (OTC:PILBF)

No significant news for the month.

Mineral Resources [ASX:MIN] (MALRF)

Mineral Resources core Australian lithium assets include the Mt. Marion Mine and the Wodgina Lithium Mine JV (ownership subject to final settlement in H1, 2026 - MinRes 35%, POSCO 15%, Albemarle 50%).

No significant news for the month.

Sigma Lithium Resources [TSXV:SGML] (SGMLF) (SGML)

Sigma is producing lithium spodumene at its Grota do Cirilo Mine in Brazil.

On January 13, Sigma Lithium announced:

Sigma Lithium sells 100,000t of high purity lithium fines; Advances mining remobilization; Appoints distinguished agribusiness leader and former minister as new board member...The purchased large-scale equipment combined with the subcontracted smaller equipment is expected to almost triple previous mining and earth-moving capacity...The Company will provide guidance for 2026 output once production resumption reaches a "steady state" during the first quarter... The steady provision of ore also has the objective to de-risk cash flow during the planned upcoming commissioning of Greentech Industrial Plant 2, which is to double lithium oxide concentrate production capacity.

Upcoming catalysts:

  • 2026 - Second line to be commissioned, doubling production capacity to 520,000tpa spodumene once ramped. A later third stage plans to expand the mine to 125,000tpa LCE.

Liontown Limited [ASX:LTR] (OTCPK:LINRF) - formerly Liontown Resources Limited

Liontown 100% owns the Kathleen Valley Lithium (spodumene) Mine in Western Australia.

On January 29, Liontown Resources announced: "December 2025 Quarterly Webcast."

AMG Critical Materials N.V. [NA:AMG] [GR:ADG] (OTCPK:AMVMF)

No news for the month.

Upcoming catalysts:

  • 2026-2028 - German LiOH facility expansion plan with Modules 2-5 (100,00tpa LiOH).

Elevra Lithium [ASX:ELV] (NASDAQ:ELVR) - Formed from a merger of Sayona Mining & Piedmont Lithium

On January 12, Elevra announced:

Accelerated NAL expansion...further refinement of the NAL mine expansion program has identified a pathway to stage permitting, construction and capital investment to fast-track additional production with lower unit operating costs. Elevra previously outlined a whole-of project expansion that would increase spodumene concentrate production to 315ktpa, with construction to be completed by the end of CY29 and commissioning thereafter...This staged development strategy is anticipated to bring forward incremental production by approximately two years compared with previous plans, while also spreading capital expenditure over a longer period.

Lithium Argentina AG [TSX:LAR](LAR) - formerly Lithium Americas Argentina

Lithium Argentina owns an effective 44.8% share of the Cauchari-Olaroz JV and also a 33% share in the Pozuelos-Pastos Grandes lithium brine project JV. The key partner in both is Ganfeng Lithium.

On January 6, Lithium Argentina announced:

2025 production results for Cauchari-Olaroz and leadership updates. 2025 Operational Highlights:

  • Achieved high-end of 2025 production guidance. Full-year 2025 production totaled approximately 34,100 tonnes of lithium carbonate.
  • Record fourth quarter production. Fourth quarter 2025 production reached approximately 9,700 tonnes, with an average operating rate of 97% nameplate capacity.
  • Cash operating costs expected to be below $6,000 per tonne. Fourth quarter 2025 cash operating costs are expected to be below $6,000 per tonne of lithium carbonate sold compared to $6,285 per tonne during the third quarter 2025, reflecting continued operational optimization and higher production volumes.
  • Strong year-end financial position. In the fourth quarter, the Cauchari-Olaroz operation is expected to reduce net debt by $26 million with $15 million distributed to both Ganfeng and Lithium Argentina and over $150 million in liquidity from cash and available undrawn debt facilities.
  • Stage 2 RIGI application submitted. In December, Cauchari-Olaroz submitted environmental permit and RIGIiii applications for a Stage 2 expansion of 45,000 tpa of lithium carbonate production capacity. In addition, the RIGI application for the Pozuelos-Pastos Grandes ("PPG") project is being finalized and expected to be submitted during the first quarter of 2026.

Upcoming catalysts:

  • 2026 - Stage 2 Cauchari-Olaroz is advancing an expansion plan considering an additional production capacity of 45,000 tpa LCE. (Stage 1 capacity is at least 35,000tpa).
  • 2026-28 - Progress on the Pozuelos-Pastos Grandes lithium brine project JV (Ganfeng 67%, LAR 33%) with a Scoping Study for up to 150,000tpa LCE.

NB: Ganfeng Lithium (51%) and Lithium Argentina (49%) own the JV company Minera Exar S.A., which owns a 91.5% interest and is entitled to 100% of the production from the Cauchari-Olaroz Project. The 8.5% interest is owned by Jujuy Energia y Mineria Sociedad del Estado ("JEMSE") (a company owned by the Government of Jujuy province).

Zijin Mining Group [SHA:601899] [HKSE:2899] (ZIJMF)

Zijin Mining is a large, diversified mining group with global mines focused on copper, gold, zinc/lead, silver, and lithium. Zijin Mining owns 100% of the 3Q Lithium Mine in Argentina, with Stage 1 production starting and producing 2,903t LCE in 2023 and planning to ramp to 25,000tpa LCE. Zijin has other lithium assets and large production growth plans.

On December 30, Zijin Mining announced:

Announcement on estimated increase in operating results for the year ending 31 December...It is estimated that the Company will realise a net profit attributable to owners of the listed company of approximately RMB51.0-52.0 billion for the year ending 31 December 2025 (the "Year 2025"), representing an increase of approximately RMB18.9-19.9 billion, or an increase of approximately 59%-62% compared with the same period last year...It is estimated that the Company will realise a net profit attributable to owners of the listed company after non-recurring profit or loss of approximately RMB47.5-48.5 billion for the Year 2025, representing an increase of approximately RMB15.8-16.8 billion, or an increase of approximately 50%-53% compared with the same period last year.

Eramet [FR:ERA] (OTCPK:ERMAY) (OTCPK:ERMAF)

Eramet owns the Centenario-Ratones Lithium Mine in Argentina.

No significant news for the month; however, Reuters reported in Dec. 2025: "Eramet eyes manganese, lithium growth in turnaround plan...may also get a profit boost from the ramp-up of its lithium production in Argentina, with capacity expected to be reached by the end of 2026."

Upcoming catalysts include:

  • End 2026 - Ramp up of Phase 1 lithium production in Argentina to full capacity of 24ktpa LCE. The long-term plan is to expand to above 75ktpa LCE.

POSCO [KRX:005490] (PKX)

POSCO owns the Sal de Oro Project in the Hombre Muerto salar, Argentina. Some details are here. POSCO also has a JV with Pilbara Minerals on a 43ktpa lithium hydroxide facility in South Korea, which commenced operations in 2024.

On December 24, POSCO Future M announced:

POSCO Future M signs Joint Venture Agreement with CNGR and FINO for LFP cathode materials...With the joint venture agreement in place, POSCO Future M will proceed with the construction of an LFP cathode material plant at Pohang's Yeongil Bay General Industrial Complex 4. The company aims to break ground in 2026 and begin mass production in 2027, with plans to expand its annual capacity to a maximum of 50,000 tons starting with this investment.

Upcoming catalysts include:

  • 2026 - Ramp up production at Hombre Muerto and ramp to 25ktpa LiOH, then later an additional 25ktpa lithium carbonate. Acquire the Hombre Muerto North Lithium Project from Lithium South Development Corp.

Core Lithium Ltd. [ASX:CXO] [GR:7CX] (CXOXF) (CORX)

Core 100% owns the Finniss Lithium Project (Grants Resource) in Northern Territory, Australia. The project is currently not in production due to previous low prices.

On January 22, Core Lithium announced:

December Quarterly Activities Report...Core enters the 2026 calendar year focused on concluding operational readiness and funding workstreams, with the objective of enabling a Final Investment Decision for Finniss and a subsequent restart of operations.

Upcoming catalysts include:

  • 2026 - Any resumption of mining operations at the Grants open pit at Finniss, where mining has been suspended since Jan. 2024.

Argosy Minerals [ASX:AGY][GR:AM1] (ARYMF)

Argosy Minerals owns 77.5% (with the option to increase to 90%) of the Rincon Lithium Mine in Argentina. Their 2,000tpa lithium facility is currently suspended.

On January 12, Argosy announced:

Significant progress with 40mw energy infrastructure to power Rincon Lithium Project...Grid connection access improves operational reliability and renewable energy integration for Rincon 12ktpa project and future expansion.

Upcoming catalysts:

  • 2026+ - Work on the next stage (total 12,000tpa) project at Rincon Lithium Project. Any reopening of the suspended 2,000tpa operations at Rincon Lithium Mine.

Other Chinese lithium producers (several are not listed)

  • Zhicun Lithium Group Ltd. (private)
  • Sichuan Yahua Industrial Group Co., Ltd. [SHE:002497]
  • Jianxiawo (owned by CATL)
  • Jiangxi Nanshi Lithium Battery New Material Co., Ltd. (owned by Nanshi Group and Yichun Mining)
  • Jinzhou Yongshan Lithium [SHA:603399]
  • Sinomine Resource Group [SHE:002738]
  • Canmax [SHE:300390]
  • Huayou Cobalt [SHA:603799]

Lithium Miner ETFs

  • Sprott Lithium Miners ETF (LITP) - A pure play lithium ETF
  • Global X Lithium & Battery Tech ETF (LIT)
  • ProShares S&P Global Core Battery Metals ETF (ION)
  • The Amplify Lithium & Battery Technology ETF (BATT)

Global X Lithium & Battery Tech ETF (LIT) 5-year price chart (source)

Trend Investing lithium demand vs. supply model forecasts

Trend Investing forecasts lithium demand to increase 10.7x this decade.

Note: A Nov. 2020 UBS forecast is for "lithium demand to lift 11-fold from ~400kt in 2021 through to 2030."

Conclusion

China lithium carbonate spot prices and spodumene spot prices surged significantly higher the past month.

Highlights for the month were:

  • The China lithium carbonate spot price surged ~60% higher last month.
  • Katusa Research is forecasting a small lithium deficit to begin in 2026.
  • CATL's key lithium mine is expected to resume production around Chinese New Year.
  • CATL confirms 2026 large-scale sodium-ion battery deployment in multiple sectors.
  • Fastmarkets: Lithium prices surge amid supply squeeze and speculative frenzy.
  • Trading Economics: Lithium carbonate futures surged past CNY 150,000/t in January. Chinese authorities lowered export rebates for battery producers from April, driving manufacturers to front-run lithium orders. Authorities stated 27 mining permits were canceled in the lithium hub of Jiangxi.
  • CATL signs a $17.2 billion LFP cathode material order with Ronbay over 5 years.
  • Argentina sets out 10-year plan for lithium growth. Possible doubling of national production capacity by the early 2030s if priority projects proceed as planned.
  • Lithium swings from glut to scarcity on Asia demand, Traxys says. Surging BESS shipments are forecast to strongly boost lithium demand.
  • China EV battery installations rise 40.4% to 769.7 GWh in 2025, LFP share at 81.2%. China's combined production of power and energy storage batteries in December reached 201.7 GWh, up 62.1% YoY.
  • US lawmakers introduce bill to create $2.5 billion critical-minerals stockpile.
  • SQM completes agreement with Codelco. Issue and sale of US$600 million subordinated capital notes.
  • Rio Tinto's 2025 lithium production was 57kt LCE (includes the contribution from Arcadium); 2026 guidance is 61-64kt.
  • Chengxin Lithium sees 2025 net loss at 600-850 million yuan.
  • Sigma Lithium advances mining remobilization in preparation for Stage 2 expansion.
  • Elevra announces accelerated NAL expansion to reach 315ktpa spodumene production.
  • Lithium Argentina Cauchari-Olaroz JV achieved the high end of 2025 production guidance with 34,100t LCE produced. Record Q4 production of 9,700t LCE.
  • Zijin Mining - Estimated 2025 net profit after non-recurring profit or loss of ~RMB 47.5-48.5 billion for the year 2025, an increase of ~50%-53% YoY.
  • POSCO Future M signs Joint Venture Agreement with CNGR and FINO for LFP cathode materials.

As usual, all comments are welcome.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Monolithic Power Systems: Premium Valuation Is Well-Deserved
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Unlike most growth investors that just look at topline performance with little regard for the bottom line, when looking at names that are growing revenue by a significant amount, I like to look at how that growth factor benefits the bottom line on a cash-generative basis. The reason for this is to avoid making speculative assumptions about how management will figure out an optimal cash-generative strategy in the future. My type of analysis leans less toward trying to predict how the future plays out and more toward observing the present and estimating a few changes going forward.

When investigating growth names with high potential to compound value for shareholders in the long run, Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR) came out on top. The main growth catalyst for MPWR growth is increasing demand for compute coming from massive adoption of AI use cases, increasing automation in manufacturing, and continuous adoption of electric vehicles, and given the structural efficiencies this company runs on, the growth will likely benefit shareholders in high dividend payouts as the majority of the revenue gain will pass through to the bottom line. I am rating MPWR a buy.

Comparative analysis

The Rule of 40

This name trades at a premium multiple, and for that reason there are a lot of investors (especially the value-leaning ones) that will immediately dismiss it after one glance at its EBITDA multiple. Some will also look at the price appreciation, assume the rally has played out, and wait for a correction to get that nice entry point; this explains the hold rating by Seeking Alpha analysts.

The rule of 40 gives us a good framework to figure out why this name deserves a premium valuation and why it could be a potential compounder going forward. Below is a list of the top 10 names within the semiconductors and electrical components industry, names I consider to be close peers to MPWR. I filtered by EBITDA multiple at a range where MPWR is trading (50-70 multiple). The Seeking Alpha screener's top 10 ranks were these names.

Looking at revenue (TTM) growth YoY, MPWR came third at 30.48%, below AMSC and AMD, and also came third in FCF Margin at 18.43%, below MCHP and VICR. On EBIT and EBITDA margins, MPWR outperforms peers with margins of 26.01% and 27.82%, respectively. Taking into consideration that the rule of 40 isn't normally applied in companies that are in the semis space, to be conservative in my analysis, I decided to apply this formula.

  • Revenue growth rate YoY + FCF Margin >= 40%

Of the 10 listed companies, those that passed the rule of 40 were

  • AMSC (55.31%)
  • MPWR (48.91%)
  • VICR (43.20%)
  • AMD (41.96%)

Among the 4 firms, MPWR had better metrics since the FCF margin is in the high teens and the revenue growth rate is in the 30s, implying that a healthy amount of revenue converts to cash for the firm and its shareholders.

Looking at the yearly return, these names massively outperformed the market, including Analog Devices (ADI), the name that didn't show up on the screen but is the closest peer to MPWR, showing investors' interest in most names in this sector. The only laggard was AMSC, which returned only 10.68%.

Profitability and growth factor

When comparing MPWR to its sectoral peers according to the Seeking Alpha Quant rating, MPWR scored an A+ for profitability and a B for growth. MPWR gross margins (TTM) sit at 55.24% vs. the sector median of 48.89%. EBIT and FCF margins sit at 26.01% and 18.43% vs. the sector median of 6.26% and 11.22%, respectively. The growth metrics are slightly less impressive due to the FCF YoY growth rate of -1.64% vs. the median growth rate of 8.34%. Revenue and EBIT growth rates were 30.48% and 42.50% vs. the median growth rates of 9.17% and 13.16%, respectively.

At a 5-year horizon, MPWR grew revenue by 185.2%, outperforming most of its peers, other than AMSC, which grew revenue by 205.6%. Looking at the EBIT margin for the last decade, we can see that MPWR maintained a margin in the 20s, with incremental expansions and less cyclicality compared to peers. The only outperformer was ADI, with margins of 45.41%. This is proof of the fact that semiconductor companies that provide more analog solutions to enterprises maintain high margins, as it's harder to commoditize analog chips than digital ones.

Financial Analysis

Another structural advantage that MPWR has is that it sources revenue from multiple end markets that are currently witnessing tremendous growth. This makes its overall growth more resilient even if one or two segments begin to witness cyclical declines. The chart below shows the topline growth of each segment on an annual basis. The enterprise data segment led growth in 2024 with an appreciation of 122% YoY to $716.26 million, but on a TTM basis, the growth has slightly declined to $663.24 million. The computing and storage segment grew 33% to $1.22 billion, and on a TTM basis, the growth increased to $1.37 billion. The automotive segment witnessed moderate 4.89% growth in 2024 to the value of $413.97 million, but on a TTM basis, the growth has significantly improved to $569.92 million. The industrial segment declined 14.68% to $147.38 million but has since recovered to $185.45 million on a TTM basis.

Q3 2025 Income Statement

Looking at the latest earnings results, revenue grew 19% YoY to $737.18 million, beating estimates by $15.04 million. The automotive segment led growth with a 36% increase, followed by the compute and storage segment, growing 30% to $186.57 million. Cost of revenue grew 20% to $330.95 million, resulting in gross profit increasing 18% to $406.23 million and landing margins at 55%. Operating expenses increased by 18%, driven by R&D and SG&A increasing by 15% and 20%, respectively. This resulted in operating income increasing to $195.18 million and landing EBIT margins at 26.47%. Net income increased 23% to $178.27 million, giving a GAAP EPS of $3.71, beating estimates by $0.42.

9-Month Income Statement

In the 9-month period, we can see revenue grew by a significant 29% to $2.04 billion. The growth was also boosted by the automotive and computing segments growing 55% and 56%, respectively, offset by enterprise data declining 10% to $486.37 million. MPWR operates in a fabless model, which lowers its capex but makes it structurally have lower margins than Texas Instruments (TXN), which in-houses manufacturing, giving it massive control over COGS. Yet we can see in the below Sankey that MPWR continues to expand margins at a healthy rate.

9-Month Cash Flow Statement

The cash flow statement clearly shows that almost all net income converts into free cash flow; the year-over-year growth rate was different, though. Operating cash flow increased 18% to $733.29 million, and free cash flow increased 10% to $428.71 million. The growth of FCF was offset by CAPEX, increasing 61% to $131 million, and SBC, increasing 16% to $173.58 million.

Q3 2025 Balance Sheet

The MPWR balance sheet is more than impressive, with cash reserved increasing 54% to $1.08 billion, surpassing total liabilities by $442.9 million. This is not surprising given the FCF margin the firm earns, which supports future dividend growth and share buybacks. The firm has zero debt, and shareholder's equity increased 52% to $3.58 billion.

Valuation

Looking at comparative valuation vs. its sector peers, MPWR earns itself a premium valuation; the Seeking Alpha Quant System gave it an F rating. Even though TTM PE looks fair at 26.24 vs. the sector median of 32.71, the multiple compression was due to the net income gain from Q4 of 2024, driven by a one-time tax benefit. Forward PE is high at 80.64 vs. the median multiple of 31.64, and TTM EV/EBITDA is at 67.87 vs. the median multiple of 19.37. This valuation is not surprising to me given that we have three segments (enterprise data, automotive, and storage) propelling topline growth with the firm's structural advantage to convert a huge amount of that revenue gain into free cash flow while maintaining zero debt in its balance sheet.

Risk

Conceptually, the stock goes up because it nets more buyers than sellers. I will admit that this high valuation is scaring a lot of investors from hitting the bid, especially given the fact that most of them really play the multiple game and don't look at the long-term potential gains to shareholders. For this reason, a large pool of investors is going to simply pass on this name, scared by a high multiple; those holding a position might want to sell, aiming to pocket some profits. From this logic, the stock might drop in the short term.

Another risk is customer concentration; in the last 10-K, the firm stated that about 17% of 2024 revenue came indirectly from a single customer (rumored to be NVIDIA). This customer has massive influence on MPWR revenue, and switching to a new product could massively impact MPWR's earnings.

Conclusion

The job of an analyst is not to tell investors that a stock trading at less than 10 PE is cheap but to tell investors that a stock trading at more than 50 PE is cheap, and in my analysis that stock is MPWR. This is a time duration argument; for the short run, I will admit the stock is fairly expensive, but in the long run, this stock has enough tailwinds to grow market penetration and its market cap to more than $100 billion. You'll simply not find names like this trading for cheap on a relative basis, so investors hoping for less than 20 P/E should wake up from that pipe dream. MPWR's growth and high FCF margin support its valuation, and more growth is to be expected in the long run; hence, I'm warranting the name a buy rating.

data.title: Williams-Sonoma: Fundamentally, It's Durable, But It's Quite Pricey
data.description:
Three months after my previous analysis, Williams-Sonoma, Inc. (WSM) sustained its bullish momentum despite my cautious stance. In fact, it made another 17% increase, which continued to prove that the stock market is non-linear. In some ways, I appreciate the optimistic market view in line with some improvements in the US economy and housing market. However, the stock is still a bit too high for my appetite and for its fundamentals and valuation. Technicals are still very bullish, but overbuying may pose some downside risks.

Williams-Sonoma: How It Has Been Recently

The US economy has shown some improvements in the past few months. However, challenges have remained evident. As a business in the consumer discretionary sector, Williams-Sonoma, Inc. is not completely shielded from these headwinds. However, its strategic business allows it to navigate the market with stability. We can see it in the most recent performance.

During Q3 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $1.88B, up by 4.6% YoY from $1.80B. This was stronger than the revenue growth of 2.7% in my previous coverage. Just looking into this, it is easy to understand why the stock kept increasing despite its seemingly overvalued price. Indeed, WSM sustained its growth, which was very impressive if you ask me. Inflation intensified during the quarter and reached its 14-month high of 3.0%. In addition, new tariffs have already been implemented. If you look at its performance per store brand, everyone was stronger on a YoY basis.

It is also interesting to note that the revenue per store averaged at $3.67M ($1.88B/513) versus $2.69B ($1.80/525). This suggests that WSM was able to raise prices or sell more despite the reduction in its store count. I also think that we can attribute it to the increase in home sales. In the same quarter, existing home sales increased by 1.5%, while supply surged by 14% YoY. While not always directly correlated, I believe that it had positive spillovers in home furnishing and kitchenware. After all, new homeowners typically mean renovation and/or new home furnishing. I also think that it can be attributed to the additional days considering the quarter-end date. But even if I adjust it, the performance will remain stronger. For instance, its daily revenue averaged $20.47M ($1.883B/92) versus $20.46M ($1.801/88) YoY.

Likewise, its COGS increased, which should not be surprising in line with inflation. However, revenue growth was more than enough to offset its increase of only 3.2%. In addition, its percentage of the combined amount of COGS and SG&A expenses decreased to 65.0% ($1.02B/($1.02B+$548.6M)) versus 65.8% YoY. This means that WSM became a bit less cost-sensitive. As a result, it maintained a double-digit operating margin at 17.0% versus 16.9% YoY.

Challenges

Inflation remains stubborn at this point. At 2.7%, it is still close to its 14-month high of 3.0%. Also, positive inflation means prices are still increasing. This can increase WSM’s cost pressures and affect consumers’ purchasing power, which can also reduce its pricing flexibility.

New tariffs may exacerbate it because WSM still imports spare parts, raw materials, and products from foreign sources like China. These added costs may have to be passed on to consumers, so it won’t fully absorb the impact. As a result, WSM may face challenges in its sales and margins.

Mitigants/Drivers

Despite these challenges, I believe that WSM is still well-positioned today in some ways.

One factor to consider is its operational efficiency. The reduction of its stores may help it manage its costs and expenses effectively. Also, it may prevent stores from predating one another. This is why I think sales still rose despite a lower store count. I also think that its operations in previous quarters were already locked in for the impact of tariffs. So, it was also able to delay and/or cushion the impact of new tariffs and rising prices. That way, it can prepare for its pricing and inventory adjustments.

Another thing I like about it is its robust liquidity. WSM has zero debt. The largest portion of its liabilities is composed of operating lease liabilities. But note that cash and cash equivalents of $884.7M can cover 60% of the amount in a single payment even if it doesn’t have to. Also, note that over 80% of the total amount will not mature within a year. Hence, it maintains an adequate capital buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

If we look at some external factors, I believe that WSM may also benefit from the recent policy easing cycle. The 75-bps rate cut in three months can make borrowings more attractive and affordable, which can support discretionary spending. Also, this can boost economic activity, which can also improve employment and wages. Aside from that, this can lower mortgage rates and make homes more accessible. Currently, the 30-year mortgage rate in the US is 6.16%, which is already close to the three-year low of 6.13%. Again, the housing market may not be directly correlated with WSM’s business operations, but positive spillovers should be considered. It is also worth noting that home sales rose by 5.1% YoY last December, the strongest in three years.

Projection

About a month before the release of the Q4 2025 results, investors may be wondering how WSM will close the year. To have a glimpse of its potential performance, I decided to make my own estimation. In line with the revenue guidance of 0.5% and 3.5% of the company, I used them as my revenue high and low. So, I estimated $8.02B and $7.75B, respectively.

Meanwhile, I set COGS (54%) and SG&A (28%) as a percentage of revenue. Instead of averaging their 2020-2024 trend, I decided to just base them on their trends in the past four quarters. Using this, their operating margin is 18.0%. In my view, this is logical since the company expects a range of 17.8% to 18.1%. Lastly, I estimated depreciation and capex using the straight-line method. So, I was also able to estimate NOPAT and EBITDA. In the end, the company will remain profitable.

Valuation

If you can remember, WSM turned out to be overvalued when I assessed it using price ratios. I also proved it using the linear regression. But this was a big mistake in my previous coverage, considering the fact that the stock has not been behaving in linear patterns towards financial metrics like the EPS and book value.

In line with that, I decided to make a forward-looking analysis using the Dividend Discount Model. Its annualized dividend is $2.64 based on the most recent quarterly payout of $0.66. Its dividend growth rate is 11.14% based on its five-year CAGR. Meanwhile, I estimated the cost of equity using the capital asset pricing model. I set its risk premium at 5.0%, which is quite a bit higher than the standard of 5.0% for a conservative estimation. Meanwhile, I estimated a Beta of 1.49 using the SLOPE function on MS Excel on the weekly price change of the stock in the past five years. I believe that my estimation is precise since it’s close to the reported Beta of 1.58 on Yahoo Finance. The difference is that it uses a monthly price change. Also, my Beta is lower, which makes sense since there should be fewer fluctuations in the stock price direction on a weekly than on a monthly basis. Using all these numbers, I derived a TP, or target price, of $196.43

The TP agrees with my view in my previous coverage that upside is limited if we consider the publication price of $184.88 versus my new TP. This justifies the increase in the stock price from my previous coverage. However, it has already increased more than necessary.

Technicals

WSM’s technicals are still very bullish as the stock price increase remains steep with higher highs and lows. It is also trending above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which means that the current momentum is still much stronger than the average momentum in the past 50 and 200 days. It may still take some time for the stock to correct. Also, the 50-day SMA is still trending upwards, which suggests that market sentiments are still generally optimistic.

Its MACD and RSI also reflect the same thing. The MACD line is still trending above the signal line in line with the increasing buying volume. The histogram is still within positive territory, which implies that buying volume still outpaces selling volume. But we can note its slight decrease from the previous week, which reflects the bears’ attempts to push the price down. Its RSI somehow gives justice to them because the stock is already overbought. This may prompt profit-takers to lighten their shares, which may lead to a dip as momentum rebalances.

Conclusion

Williams-Sonoma, Inc. remains solid with its robust performance and strategic operations despite several headwinds. This is supported by its robust fundamentals, which allow it to sustain its operating capacity and even take advantage of the recent interest rate cuts. However, valuation is already too high for me to enter at this level, with dividends promising little yields. Technicals are still bullish but present new downside risks as the stock becomes overbought. I’m reiterating my hold rating on this stock.

data.title: Essential Utilities: When Boring Becomes Profitable
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

Utility sector has always been the go-to place for investors who search for predictable returns.

For perspective, demand is always constant because people need water, wastewater, and gas. Besides that, the regulation allows the company to earn reliable profits. This whole phenomenon is brought to life by Essential Utilities (WTRG).

Essential supplies water, wastewater, and gas to millions and expand as a result of smart investments and approved rate increases. A case in point is Q3, which recorded a significant growth in its water and gas segments.

Moreover, each dollar Essential invests in its systems today will translate into revenue in the future. The management continues to increase dividends while investing in the business to ensure stable growth.

In addition, the merger with American Water Works Company (AWK) deserves attention. Naturally, merging two large companies has some risks, but if it succeeds, the growth will accelerate faster than the usual rate.

Furthermore, WTRG stock is trading at a fair value relative to the sector median. The market prices in stable performance but underestimates the upside from the merger and future rate increments.

Overall, WTRG is a 'buy' right now and has the potential to provide consistent growth with upside.

Q3 2025: Consistency Still Wins

Essential’s Q3 performance is commendable as it performed across the board.

It generated $477 million of revenue, a rise of almost 10% YOY. Profits jumped as well. Net income increased by an incredible 32.7% annually and clocked in at $92 million, or $0.33 per share.

The water business did its share of heavy lifting and contributed a whopping $364 million in revenue, an increase of nearly 9%. The gas segment came in next at $108 million, up over 12% YOY.

This growth was a result of sanctioned rate upgrades rather than mere usage changes. Meanwhile, Essential managed to keep the costs down and it worked. This is what enabled the margins to appear exactly where you would expect for a regulated utility.

Interestingly, the company still invests back into the system. During the initial 9 months of 2025, Essential spent $983 million, and it predicts to invest between $1.4 to $1.5 billion for the entire year.

Moving further ahead, the management has planned $7.8 billion of infrastructure investment between 2025 and 2029. Those investments are important as regulators permit Essential to make a reasonable profit, typically within 8-10% on each new asset added to the rate base.

Consequently, the current expenditure becomes the future revenue. The regulated rate base is anticipated to increase at a rate of 6-8% per year, which drives consistent and predictable growth in earnings. Essential’s CEO Chris Franklin claimed on the Q3 earnings call:

Our business and its economic model have proven resilient through economic booms and downturns, as well as through political and regulatory changes.

Most importantly, Essential's dividend profile is indicative of the same discipline that characterizes the business. The stock provides a forward yield of approximately 3.5% and an annual payout of $1.37/share. It's backed by a payout ratio of 60%, which leaves an opportunity to reinvest and grow in the future.

The dividend has climbed at an impressive 6.6% annual rate over the past half decade, and the company has increased its dividend for 34 consecutive years. The most recent quarterly dividend is at $0.34 per share, which is paid on a regular quarterly basis.

To be honest, this is an overall reliable income source. It is supported by controlled cash flows and a management team that is clearly focused on long-term dividend growth.

Earnings Boosted by Regulation

Regulation continues to work in favor of Essential, and it is reflected in earnings clearly.

The company has already secured good rate increments. The water and wastewater business contributed an estimated $93 million in annual revenue in various states by early November 2025, with gas contributing around $9 million. To add to that, some rate cases are still pending and can possibly generate more revenues if approved.

These wins are important, as rate gains go directly to the bottom line. Each $10 million in new rates can contribute between $0.02 and $0.03 to EPS, which makes each case much more effective than organic growth on its own.

Earnings momentum is strong even prior to counting pending cases. Essential posted full-year 2024 GAAP EPS of $2.17, up 17% YOY, and the management restated 2025 guidance somewhere between $2.07 and $2.11. These perspectives already comprise planned capital spending and known regulatory results. If rest of the cases work their way, the EPS may surpass expectations, demonstrating how regulation acts as an in-built cash flow booster.

In addition to rates, other consistent growth drivers are acquisitions. Essential has increased its water and wastewater rate base by $548 million, adding about 136,000 customers since 2015. In 2025 alone, it made deals that served a little more than 10,000 customers. It has also signed contracts in Pennsylvania and Texas, which can bring approximately 200,000 more, including the massive Delcora sewer purchase.

Although such additions are minor in comparison to the total base, they gradually increase the revenue, FCF and the long-term rate base.

Essential Taps American for Growth

Here’s the big thing: an all-stock merger with American Water.

In the deal, every Essential share will be converted into nearly 0.305 AWK shares, which is a 10% premium. Combined, the company would serve 4.7 million water and wastewater connections and have approximately $29.3 billion in rate base.

Additionally, the key shareholders would be in possession of about 31% of the new company. Hence, they get exposure to the wider footprint and credit profile of AWK. This scale definitely has the potential of increasing regulatory credibility and establishing operating leverage by joint overhead and operations.

Moreover, the management anticipates that the merger will instantly increase EPS and dividends. They also intend to maintain AWK's long-term 7-9% EPS and dividend growth targets. The two companies will keep on paying their existing dividends until the deal is closed, and that adds stability for shareholders.

The merger also comes with complementary advantages. For example, Peoples Gas of Essentials serves 705,000 customers in Pennsylvania and is expanding by more than 10% a year. This brings in a fast-expanding regulated gas business. In the meantime, access to the greater capital base of Americans could reduce the cost of financing new projects. The management also recognizes cross-selling and cost-saving opportunities but they have yet to provide exact numbers on synergy.

Naturally, the merger comes with its risks (as will be discussed in the ‘risks to my thesis’ section of this piece). That said, this merger could be a superb catalyst and can generate value that Essential would have struggled to create alone.

Valuation Reflects Stability While Upside Builds

WTRG stock experienced a decent 12.6% rise over the last year. It began at $34-$35 at the start of 2025, reached just over $40 in October, and is currently trading at around $39.

The stock's plateau at $39 shows the market weighing two large factors, which are pending rate cases and the American Water merger. Investors are indicating that they prefer consistent cash flows as opposed to short-term frenzy. This basically means that the market’s psychology is to reward stability and regulatory certainty, which is precisely what Essential provides.

The future EPS forecasts give a clearer picture. Analysts project $2.12 in 2025 and $2.23 in 2026, with revenue expected to improve from $2.37 billion to $2.44 billion. At the current price, the non-GAAP forward P/E is 18.76x, a bit lower than the sector median, which indicates stable growth. This is where rate base comes in. Every approved investment contributes to the regulated asset base, making 8-10% returns, which compound earnings on a predictable basis.

If Essential keeps implementing its projects, increasing its rate base, and closing small acquisitions, EPS may move toward the upper end of projections. That will give a reasonable base-case price range of $38 to $41 in the upcoming 12 months, with dividends serving as a reliable ground for total returns.

The bull case is based on catalysts that the market is keenly monitoring. If regulators grant more rate cases, EPS can go beyond $2.23. Add that to the American Water merger, and forward P/E would trend in the low-19x range, pushing the stock to $42-44.

This situation indicates not only the earnings increase but also the rewards that come with increased scale and credibility in the market.

Risks

For starters, the regulatory risk is the most critical. Rate-case approvals and cost recoveries are vital in the growth of Essential. Delays or rejections would decrease the estimated revenues, and a loss of $10-$20 million in approved rates would decrease EPS by a few cents. That would have a direct impact on cash flow and the capability of the company to finance capital projects per year or maintain dividend growth.

Speaking of financing, Essential plans to spend $1.4-1.5 billion on infrastructure yearly, with total investments of about $7.8 billion over the period of 2029. FCF might be strained if regulators push back on cost recovery or financing costs increase. Even with approvals, funding such projects either by debt or equity raises interest and dilution risk that may restrain operational flexibility and slow the growth in dividends.

Additionally, the merger with American Water has performance and integration risks. The transaction requires shareholder approval and regulatory approval in various states. Delay and conditions may decrease the expected upside.

The merger of two big utilities is complicated. The systems, workforces, and IT platforms might cause friction and slow execution. Even small setbacks would postpone synergies and subject short-term EPS growth to pressure.

Last but not least, short-term earnings could also be influenced by other factors like extreme weather events, temporary fall in demand, or political resistance against rate increases. Although the company possesses mechanisms to recover costs in the long run, timing gaps may create short-term pressure.

Takeaway

Here’s the truth: WTRG is the type of stock that silently works for you.

It has stable growth, a robust dividend, and a regulation that ensures earnings remain predictable. Moreover, the Q3 results, recent rate wins, and the merger with American Water mean that the management performs well and thinks about long-term growth.

Its stock is priced fairly, and future rate approvals and merger synergies provide it with a meaningful upside. For anyone seeking reliable growth and a consistent income stream, buying WTRG is a prudent choice. It creates value in the long run and lets you focus on all other things without concern.

data.title: Warner Bros. Discovery: Time To Consider An Exit Strategy (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Every party has to come to an end. It looks like it is time to assess the post-split-up value of the original AT&T (T) purchase that resulted in the situation now with Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD).

Two Strategies

In the case of Warner Bros Discovery, I followed two strategies. I had originally suggested that conservative investors take some profits after the AT&T sale to Warner Bros Discovery and the receipt of Warner Bros Discovery shares. That would have resulted in a roughly 20% profit immediately. For those that happened to keep the shares purchased, I made about 30% plus the dividend paid on my money, keeping the whole deal until now over a four-year period. But the Warner Bros. Discovery shares were up about 25% (with no dividend) very roughly in the post-split time period of four years. While any gain is better than a loss, that 3- to 4-year gain (roughly) was certainly nothing to write home about on the Warner Bros. Discovery hold idea.

However, in both cases, when the companies ran into issues. I also purchased some shares of both later. In the case of Warner Bros Discovery, those shares were purchased at (slightly) less than $11 per share, which made for a very nice gain at current prices. With financially strong companies, this points out the success of the averaging down strategy. But you have to have some faith in the strategy that management chooses when it runs into unexpected issues, or things can go south pretty fast.

Doing this purchase later turned a mediocre at best original return into something decent overall.

Exit Strategies

But now it is time to consider exiting Warner Bros Discovery common shares. The reason for this is the takeover offer by Netflix (NFLX) covered in the last article. The takeover offer essentially caps the appreciation potential in the near future.

Since that article, the offer has been amended to become an all-cash offer. While that is not much of a change, it is what we are dealing with in forming an exit strategy.

Conservative investors may want to sell now. The reason for that is there is now a sure thing. The cash received now can be invested elsewhere for another investment idea (with hopefully good results). Sometimes these deals come undone. Conservative investors may not want to take that risk.

On the other hand, there is a possibility that a bidding war could erupt after a conservative sale is made. Chart Industries (GTLS) received an offer from Baker Hughes (BKR) that was acceptable a fair amount of time after it had accepted another offer. So late offers happen, and management can change its mind.

On the other hand, an investor can choose to hold until the Netflix offer is received, and the remainder of the company business is spun off to holders. This is very roughly 10% to 15% above the current price. Whether it is worth holding until this happens is up to the investor. At what point the stock price climbs close enough that it is worth not holding on anymore is a personal decision.

For me, I am considering selling because I think that at this point it is no longer worth it for me to hold until the whole deal closes and is complete. But that may not be everyone's choice. That makes this now a hold because the future is finite (in the upwards direction) and probably a good deal lower than if the company had stayed independent.

Buying Netflix

There is a possibility that in the future I may well purchase Netflix because I believe that the combination materially improves its profitability model.

However, right now the stock is in a downtrend. I may therefore consider purchasing long-dated calls or even leaps because I do believe that the stock is priced way ahead of what the value will be in the future, given management's guidance of growth in the teens annually for some time now.

Generally, the price-earnings ratio should be one times the growth rate. It now looks like the stock price is beginning to adjust to that, as the stock price is down substantially from its high point.

It is quite possible that the acquisition offer made by Netflix was a wake-up call for the market to realize that the Netflix model needed considerable upgrading to remain independent and compete with the competition that has entered the market.

I believe that Netflix had a serious deficiency in that it basically made money from streaming, whereas the competition had movies, games, and even theme parks (among other things). Therefore, the competition could make money in more than one way off the content. This provided a profitability advantage that Netflix needed.

After the acquisition, the assimilation process will be significant in that the Netflix content needs to become monetized in more than one way in addition to what Warner Bros Discovery is already monetizing.

Netflix management has long stated that the Warner Bros. Discovery management will remain as this is by and large not businesses that Netflix is in (except for streaming). Therefore, it makes sense to allow experienced management to continue and learn from them. That should ensure merger success and make the assimilation process relatively seamless.

Summary

I tend to wait until things calm down whenever there is a merger in the future. Maybe I do not receive the best stock price. But I do follow the Warren Buffett maxim about buying low enough that even a mediocre sale brings about a decent result.

As a result, I have learned the value of being able to average down if that opportunity presents itself. If the stock price heads upwards after a merger, there is always the possibility of averaging up (which can be just as profitable but is far less discussed).

Because of all the possibilities, I consider Warner Bros Discovery a hold even though I likely will sell in the near future. There are too many investor profiles out there for one strategy to make everyone happy. Plus, the future is really unknown. The risk assessment for the situation has got to be a personal evaluation of the possibilities and ownership of what happens.

Risks

The risk largely depends upon the pathway taken in the future. A decision to hold onto the stock until the deal closes risks the deal coming undone and an immediate stock price decline from current levels. I happen to believe in the future of an independent Warner Bros. Discovery, so the potential possibility that the deal comes undone, and the company remains independent is not much of a threat.

On the other hand, sellers now risk that a better offer will come along and they will not have the stock to participate in that better offer if it is accepted by management. Right now, I consider this risk pretty small. But then again, the future is largely unknown.

There are merger conditions that have to be met, and some permissions are needed. These could prove to be insurmountable, which would cause the deal to fall apart.

Overall, though, it looks like things have settled down enough that it is time to plan an exit strategy. Let me know below what you decide.

data.title: Medallion Financial Is Consistently Delivering Strong Returns
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Medallion Financial (MFIN) has been growing its asset base consistently over the last few years and has been earning an excess return over its cost of equity. While close to stable growth, I estimate that Medallion Financial still has a few years to continue earning a premium over its cost of equity. Its net interest margin (the difference between its interest income and interest expense) has generally increased from 6.82% in 2021 to 7.33% in the trailing twelve months (“TTM”) ended September 30, 2025 (the “TTM period”). Its revenue growth minus non-interest expense growth has been in the double digits from 2021 to 2024 (with the exception of 2022, when it was 9.36%) and ended the TTM period at 10.77%. The company also reinstated its quarterly dividend at $0.08 in the first quarter of 2022, after which it increased the quarterly dividend to $0.10 in the fourth quarter of 2023 and increased it again to $0.12 in the second quarter of 2025. MFIN currently has a forward annual payout of $0.48 and a forward yield of 4.58%. Finally, the company has strong insider ownership of approximately 20%.

Company Overview

Medallion Financial is a specialty finance firm that originates and services both consumer loans (recreational vehicle loans and home improvement loans) and commercial loans, as well as also offering loan origination services to fintech strategic partners. It operates primarily through its two subsidiaries, Medallion Bank (MBNKO), which offers consumer loans, and Medallion Capital, which offers commercial loans.

Medallion Bank opened in December 2003 in Salt Lake City, Utah, to support its parent company’s taxicab medallion lending business. They ceased originating taxicab medallion loans in late 2014, and the bank and its parent company have since wound down the vast bulk of that portfolio to only $5.1 million in net assets as of September 30, 2025. The bank now offers consumer loans on recreational vehicles and boats, as well as loans for the purchase of home improvements. Since 2020, the bank has also offered strategic partnerships via banking-as-a-service lending. Medallion Financial is the sole owner of all of Medallion Bank’s common stock. There are currently two preferred classes of stock at the bank, one of which, the Series G preferred stock, has traded publicly as MBNKO since May 22, 2025. I previously covered this security in another article for Seeking Alpha. The other series of preferred stock is the Series E Preferred Stock, of which 26,303 shares with a liquidation amount of $1,000 per share are outstanding with the U.S. Treasury under the Small Business Lending Fund Program.

Medallion Capital handles Medallion Financial’s commercial lending segment, targeting lower middle market companies in the U.S. Investments range from $2.5 million to $6.0 million. Medallion Capital is a Small Business Investment Company (“SBIC”), which provides debt and equity financing to small businesses and startups while being regulated by the U.S. Small Business Administration.

As of September 30, 2025, Medallion Financial’s book value of shareholders’ equity has grown from 2021 at a compound annual growth rate ("CAGR") of 12.07%. Its book value per share is $17.07, and its tangible book value (book value less goodwill and intangible assets) per share is $9.82. Per management, this tangible book value per share should be adjusted upwards by $1.82 if it includes a correlated deferred tax liability associated with goodwill and intangible assets. This would result in an adjusted tangible book value per share of $11.64, well above the levels MFIN is trading at currently.

Ratio Analysis

A look at some historical ratios (calculated using Medallion Financial’s 10-Ks and 10-Qs) gives an indication of how Medallion Financial has performed. A series of ratios on profitability, financial strength, and efficiency, all using figures as reported, are shown in the following table.

Net Interest Margin measures the difference between interest income and interest expenses, with a higher margin being better. Medallion Financial has generally grown its net interest margin from 6.82% in 2021 to 7.33% in the TTM period.

Return on Equity (“ROE”) shows how much net income is generated by the common shareholder’s equity (note, net income is what remains after income attributable to the non-controlling interest is paid out). While this has generally declined over the years reviewed, it was still a respectable 11.31% for the TTM period and higher than its cost of equity. Please note, for consistency with some of the sources used, ROE is calculated using the prior year’s book value of common shareholders’ equity (not the average of the prior year’s and current year’s book value of equity).

Return on Assets (“ROA”) measures net income against total assets of Medallion Financial. This measure also generally declined from 3.29% in 2021 to 1.42% in the TTM period. Please note, for consistency with some of the sources used, ROA is calculated using the prior year’s book value of assets (not the average of the prior year’s and current year’s book value of assets).

The provision for credit losses ratio shows the amount of provisions for credit losses as a percentage of net loans. This number increased from 3.49% in 2021 to 4.68% in the TTM period. The provision for credit losses measure also reduces net income and increases the allowance for credit losses, a contra-asset account on the balance sheet.

Several ratios for financial strength were reviewed, with higher percentages showing greater financial strength. The Tier 1 leverage ratio shows a bank’s core capital to its total assets. Common Equity Tier 1 (“CET1”) risk-based capital ratio shows a bank’s common equity (i.e., exclusive of preferred stock and qualifying minority interests) against its risk-weighted assets. Tier 1 risk-based capital ratio shows a bank's equity capital and disclosed reserves against its total risk-weighted assets. Finally, the total risk-based capital ratio shows the sum of Tier 1 and Tier 2 capital divided by the bank’s risk-weighted assets. All of these measures are significantly above regulatory minimums for all years reviewed, including the TTM period.

The efficiency ratio shows non-interest expenses per dollar of revenue, with a lower ratio being better. This ratio gradually decreased over the years reviewed, from 45.74% in 2021 to 33.34% in the TTM period.

Revenue growth less non-interest expense growth, when positive, shows that revenue is growing faster than expenses, as opposed to a negative ratio, which shows the company is accumulating expenses faster than it is growing revenues. This figure generally declined from 35.85% in 2021 to 10.77% in the TTM period, with a low of 9.36% in 2022.

In summary, all of these ratios show Medallion Financial has historically had solid performance and a consistent ability to more than adequately meet its regulatory capital requirements and still be able to pay out dividends.

Adjustments to Historical Net Income, ROE & ROA

Using Medallion Financial's 10-Ks and 10-Qs, the company’s historical net income, ROE, and ROA from 2021 through the TTM period were reviewed. These figures, including the subtractions from net income after taxes of income attributable to the non-controlling interest (dividends to the two outstanding series of preferred stock at Medallion Bank) and the redemption of non-controlling interests in the TTM period (redemption of the Series F Preferred Stock of Medallion Bank), are shown in the following table.

Historical net income ranged from a low of $35.88 million in 2024 to a high of $55.08 million in 2023, with an average of $45.98 million for all periods. ROE averaged 15.74% across all periods, and ROA averaged 2.18% across all periods.

In the next table, labeled Adjusted Net Income, ROE, & ROA, the historical net income attributable to the common shareholder (as reported) is shown at the top. To better compare historical periods’ net income with the recent material increase in the annual preferred dividends paid out for the newly issued Series G Preferred Stock, I made adjustments to net income attributable to the common shareholder.

Starting with net income attributable to the common shareholder, the historical income attributable to the non-controlling interest was added back for all periods reviewed, and the redemption of a non-controlling interest in the TTM period was added back. I then subtracted from all periods the current annual preferred dividends for the Series E Preferred Stock and subtracted the annual preferred dividends for the new Series G Preferred Stock under adjusted income attributable to the non-controlling interest (Bank Series G).

There were also two factors included in 2021 that are not present in any other year, so these were adjusted out to make 2021’s net income more comparable with the later periods. The $4.63 million gain on extinguishment of debt was subtracted from 2021’s net income, as was the $12.57 million in net sponsorship & race winnings (from a previously owned professional race team disposed of in 2021). I also added back the race team-related expenses of $9.56 million from 2021.

Finally, in December 2024, there was a one-time expense of a civil penalty of $3.0 million due to a settlement agreement with the SEC resolving outstanding litigation. Please see Risks & Other Factors for a more detailed description. I adjusted this one-time penalty expense out of fiscal year 2024 and the TTM period's net income.

These changes resulted in the adjusted net income attributable to the common shareholder, as shown in the following table.

Adjusted net income ranged from a low of $35.58 million in 2024 to a high of $51.78 million in 2023, with an average of $42.94 million across all periods. Adjusted ROE averaged 14.39% across all periods, and adjusted ROA averaged 1.98% across all periods. Adjusted earnings per share (diluted) for the TTM period is $1.91, compared to $1.70 per share (diluted) for the unadjusted net income attributable to the common shareholder.

Excess Return Model

An excess return model calculates the value of a firm by summing capital currently invested and the present value of excess returns that the firm is expected to make in the future. This model is ideal for financial services companies with complex and uncertain cash flows. To accurately calculate the present value of future returns, several assumptions and inputs need to be decided upon.

Given the average adjusted net income of $42.94 million was comparable to the TTM period's adjusted net income of $46.12 million, I used the TTM period's adjusted net income as the base net income for the model and used the book value of equity of $362.39 million at the start of the TTM period (i.e., October 1, 2024) as the base level book value of equity used to calculate an ROE of 12.73%. Adjusted earnings per share for the TTM period (adjusted net income divided by the weighted average common shares outstanding on a diluted basis, or 24.1 million shares) of $1.91 and annual dividends per share of $0.48 were used in the model.

To calculate the discount rate, or cost of equity, I used the Build-Up Method and the Capital Asset Pricing Model (“CAPM”). I selected a beta of 0.68 (provided by Yahoo Finance using 5 years of monthly returns), an equity risk premium of 4.23% for 2025 (as provided by Professor Aswath Damodaran, a professor at Stern School of Business at New York University), and a risk-free rate of 4.275% (the 10-year Treasury note). Given the market capitalization of MFIN is $229.9 million, placing it roughly in the 9th decile of all publicly traded stocks ranked by market capitalization, I also added an estimated small stock risk premium of 2.5% to arrive at a cost of equity of approximately 9.65%.

Since the adjusted net incomes of the review period were relatively similar, I estimated a length of a high-growth period for Medallion Financial of three years, with a starting ROE of 12.73% (as previously described) and the book value of equity of $397.45 million as of September 30, 2025. The retention rate of 74.87% was estimated by taking 1 minus the current dividends per share divided by the current earnings per share.

Over the 3-year high-growth period, I adjusted the ROE downwards gradually to 9.75% in the terminal year. I obtained this ROE from Professor Aswath Damodaran’s research showing an average ROE of 9.75% for regional banks (the closest comparable industry available) as of January 2026. I also adjusted the retention ratio downwards from 74.87% to 30.77% in the terminal year. Finally, I gradually adjusted the cost of equity upwards from 9.65% to 11.01% in the terminal year (driven by increasing the beta from 0.68 to 1.00 at the period of stable growth of the terminal year).

This resulted in excess equity returns of $12.22 million, $7.10 million, and negative $5.85 million for future years one through three. The third year is negative as that year is when the cost of equity of 11.01% exceeds the stable-growth period’s ROE of 9.75%. The terminal value also uses this cost of equity and ROE, resulting in a terminal value of negative $75.26.

The present value of these returns, as discounted using the cumulated cost of equity (which considers the gradual growth in cost of equity), is $11.15 in year one, $5.88 in year two, and negative $60.52 in year three (inclusive of the terminal value). These sum to equal a present value of equity excess returns of negative $43.49, as shown in the following tables.

When added to the $397.45 of equity invested on September 30, 2025, and then divided by the number of shares outstanding (on a diluted basis), it results in an estimated value per share of $14.67, or $4.82 higher than today’s share price of $9.85. This implies an upside of 48.9% from current levels.

Risks & Other Factors

There are some risks to factor into the purchase of MFIN, as detailed in the company’s most recent 10-K. Primary among them is a rapid shift in interest rates. Per its latest 10-K, the market risk of an immediate increase in interest rates of 1% over a one-year period would be a reduction in net income of $2.7 million at December 31, 2024. A decrease in interest rates, while contributive to the value of outstanding loans, could raise reinvestment risk as amounts repaid on existing loans could not be reinvested at the same rates previously obtained. Additionally, the risk of early refinancing of outstanding loans increases as market interest rates decrease. There is a risk that the allowance for credit losses may prove to be insufficient to cover loan losses. There is a risk that financial covenants under debt arrangements could be breached, a default of which could accelerate related and other debt to immediate repayment. Due to its structure as a holding company, Medallion Financial is dependent on cash flows from its subsidiaries to make payments on debts and to fund operations. As stated in the most recent 10-Q (September 30, 2025),

"on December 29, 2021, the SEC filed a civil complaint in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York against the Company and its President and Chief Operating Officer alleging certain violations of the antifraud, books and records, internal controls and anti-touting provisions of the federal securities laws.

...

On May 30, 2025, the Court entered a Final Judgment as to the Company and its President and Chief Operating Officer resolving this litigation. The parties agreed to settle the matter with the SEC, consenting to the entry of the Final Judgment, without admitting or denying the allegations of the SEC complaint. Pursuant to the Final Judgment, among other things, (i) the parties were enjoined from violating specified provisions of the federal securities laws and rules thereunder, (ii) the Company paid a civil penalty of $3,000,000 (which amount was previously accrued in the fourth quarter of 2024) and (iii) the Company agreed to certain compliance-related undertakings."

The company does not use any AI tools or models in critical areas of its business, and it is not aware of any third parties that use AI tools or models in a manner that would materially impact Medallion Financial’s business, but this might change in the future. The company had previously suspended its dividend (during the height of its taxi medallion lending crisis) and only recently brought it back. Finally, the company’s concentration in consumer lending typically carries a risk of loss that is higher than commercial lending and can be adversely affected by an economic downturn. The likelihood of an economic downturn increases substantially when trade wars heat up, as they are currently, or if the independence of the Federal Reserve comes into question.

Conclusion

Given all of the above, I believe the potential upside of MFIN far outweighs its risks. The company has mostly overcome the stigma of its former taxi medallion lending business and has a bright future ahead of it. My estimated value per share of $14.67 using an excess return model reflects that potential upside of 48.9%.

data.title: Antero Resources: Projected FCF Boosted By Recent Transactions
data.description:
Antero Resources (AR) is increasing its production by approximately 20% through its recent transactions and may end up with around 4.2+ Bcfe per day in pro forma 2026 production.

The transactions add around $2 billion to Antero's debt, but it is also expecting close to $500 million in net additional 2026 free cash flow from the transactions. I now project Antero to generate around $1.54 billion in pro forma 2026 free cash flow at the current strip if it goes with a maintenance capex budget.

I have increased my estimate of Antero's value by approximately $1.50 per share to a new estimate of $39 to $40 per share. This is based on long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

Recent Transactions

Antero Resources announced a couple of significant transactions in December 2025. It acquired HG Energy II's upstream assets (West Virginia Marcellus) for $2.8 billion in cash plus the assumption of HG Energy's hedge book. That hedge book is reasonably close to neutral value, although slightly negative at the current strip.

Antero Resources is also divesting its Ohio Utica Shale upstream assets for $800 million in cash.

These transactions are expected to net out to approximately 700 MMCFE per day in additional 2026 production for Antero Resources for a total net cost of $2 billion.

Antero also estimated that its 2026 EBITDAX would increase by approximately $700 million and its 2026 free cash flow would increase by approximately $495 million due to these transactions. This was based on early December 2025 strip prices, which were above $4 for NYMEX natural gas (a bit above the current $3.95 level).
